statement.markdown: The N-Queens II problem is a classic backtracking puzzle, but the given code seems to have a bug. The code aims to count the number of distinct arrangements of N queens on an NxN chessboard such that no two queens attack each other. However, the code only correctly counts the number of arrangements for boards up to size 4. Write a corrected version of the method, using backtracking, to find the total number of distinct arrangements of N queens on an NxN board.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a single integer n, representing the size of the chessboard (n x n).
statement.outputFormat: The output should be the total number of distinct arrangements of N queens on the chessboard.
statement.notes: Use backtracking to explore all possible placements of the queens.

constraints.description: Size of the chessboard (n x n)

examples.explanation: There are 2 possible arrangements of 4 queens on a 4x4 board such that no two queens attack each other.

hints.text: Recall that a queen can attack another queen if she is in the same row, column, or diagonal.

editorial.title: N-Queens II: Backtracking Bug Fix
editorial.intuition: The given code only counts the number of arrangements for boards of size up to 4. The key to solving this problem lies in using backtracking to correctly count the number of distinct arrangements of N queens on an NxN chessboard, regardless of the board size.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves trying all possible placements of the queens and checking if they are valid. However, this approach is inefficient and will not work for larger board sizes.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution involves using backtracking to explore all possible placements of the queens.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using backtracking to correctly count the number of distinct arrangements of N queens on an NxN chessboard, regardless of the board size.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution lies in the use of backtracking to explore all possible placements of the queens and checking if each placement is valid.
editorial.dryRun:
For a board size of 4, the placements would be: 
[1, 3, 0, 2]
[2, 0, 3, 1]
[3, 1, 0, 2]
[3, 2, 1, 0]
[2, 0, 3, 1]
[3, 1, 0, 2]
[0, 1, 2, 3]
[2, 3, 0, 1]
[1, 0, 3, 2]
[0, 2, 3, 1]
[1, 3, 0, 2]
[3, 0, 2, 1]
The total number of valid arrangements is 2, as specified in the example.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly counting the number of valid arrangements
Failing to use backtracking to explore all possible placements of the queens
